About me

Curiosity as a
methodology.

Hello! I'm Soham Sawant, a Physics student and independent researcher from India with a strong interest in nuclear physics, particle physics, plasma physics, and experimental science. Driven by a fascination with the universe's deepest mysteries, I am particularly interested in probing the fundamental constituents of nature and the laws that govern them.

My academic journey began in engineering, where I developed an appreciation for design, instrumentation, and practical problem-solving. While engineering provided me with the tools to understand and build complex systems, it also sparked a deeper curiosity about the physical principles that underlie them. This curiosity ultimately led me to transition into physics, where I could explore these questions from a more fundamental perspective while retaining the hands-on mindset that engineering fostered.

Today, my interests lie at the intersection of theory and experiment. I am particularly drawn to scientific instrumentation and the process of transforming physical ideas into tangible systems that can be built, tested, and refined. Through independent projects and research-oriented work, I continue to explore physics not only as a body of knowledge, but as a discipline best understood through observation, experimentation, and direct engagement with the natural world.

Research & Projects

Selected work.

A collection of research projects, computational experiments, and written explorations across physics and engineered systems.

Digital Radiation Detection Instrumentation System
Radiation Physics & Nuclear Instrumentation 2025
Designed and constructed a Geiger–Müller-based radiation detection system capable of detecting ionizing radiation and displaying real-time count and radiation dosage data through an integrated LCD interface. The project involved high-voltage electronics, signal processing, detector interfacing, and radiation measurement instrumentation.

Publications

Published research.

01
Radiation Physics & Nuclear Instrumentation
Enhanced Ionizing Radiation Detection using a Geiger–Müller Counter Integrated with Data Acquisition System
Soham Sawant, Kumar Harsh Anand
A compact, low-cost system integrating a GM counter with an Arduino-based data acquisition unit and LCD display. Background radiation levels were recorded, converted to dose rates, and analyzed statistically — confirming Poisson fluctuations in radioactive decay.
2025 View ›
Full Summary

Abstract

Ionizing radiation is a natural and artificial environmental factor requiring accurate monitoring for research, education, and safety. This work presents a compact, low-cost system built by integrating a Geiger–Müller counter with an Arduino-based data acquisition unit and LCD display. Background radiation levels were recorded, converted to dose rates, and analyzed statistically. Results confirmed Poisson fluctuations in radioactive decay and demonstrated consistent detector performance.

Introduction & Background

Ionizing radiation originates from natural sources (cosmic rays, terrestrial isotopes) and artificial sources (medical, nuclear). Its detection is essential for safety, education, and research. The Geiger–Müller (GM) counter is a gas-filled detector where incoming radiation ionizes the medium, producing an avalanche of electrons that generates a measurable pulse — expressed as counts per minute (CPM). Radiation intensity decreases with distance following the inverse-square law, and attenuation in matter follows an exponential decay. Counts obey Poisson statistics.

Experimental Results

Background CPM counts across six trials ranged from 13 to 22 CPM. Estimated dose rates fell between 0.074–0.125 µSv/h. Poisson error propagation was applied across measurement intervals of 60s, 90s, and 120s.

TrialCPMDose Rate (µSv/hr)
1140.0798
2220.1254

IntervalCountsCPM ± σDose Rate (µSv/h ± σ)
60s1111 ± 3.320.0627 ± 0.0189
90s2013.3 ± 2.980.0760 ± 0.0170
120s2512.5 ± 3.160.0712 ± 0.0180

Discussion & Conclusion

Background counts averaged 15.8 CPM, consistent with Poisson statistics. The dose rate showed a gradual increase over time, suggesting subtle variations in ambient radiation — potentially influenced by cosmic ray flux or environmental activity. Graphical analysis revealed occasional clustering of counts, showing that natural radiation occurs in microbursts rather than being uniform. Comparison with simulated Poisson distributions confirmed the detector's reliability and sensitivity.

Authors
